specification.topic-statement: The generic array is a type-agnostic resizable collection that stores fixed-size elements contiguously and grows its capacity on demand.

scope.in-scope: Array creation, element appending, element insertion, element deletion, element replacement, element access, size and capacity queries, and the byte-typed specialization of the generic array.
scope.boundary: This topic allocates memory and panics on allocation failure. Memory ownership follows the talloc hierarchy — the array and its buffer are children of the context provided at creation. Freeing the context frees the array and buffer.

field.name: element size
field.note: bytes per element; must be greater than zero

field.name: increment
field.note: capacity for first allocation; must be greater than zero

field.name: size
field.note: current number of elements; starts at zero

field.name: capacity
field.note: allocated capacity in elements; starts at zero

field.name: buffer
field.note: null until first insertion or append

behavior.creation: A new array is created by supplying an element size and an increment value. If either value is zero, creation fails with an invalid-argument error and no array is returned. On success, the array starts with zero elements, zero capacity, and no allocated buffer. No buffer is allocated at creation time.
behavior.appending:
An element is appended to the end of the array. If the current number of elements equals the current capacity, the array grows before the element is copied. The element is copied by value using the element size specified at creation. The element count increases by one. The operation always succeeds — any allocation failure terminates the process.
When the array is full, capacity is expanded. If the current capacity is zero, the new capacity equals the increment. Otherwise the new capacity doubles. The buffer is reallocated to hold the new capacity. Allocation failure terminates the process immediately.
behavior.insertion: An element is inserted at a specified index. The index must be between zero and the current element count inclusive — inserting at the end is valid. If the array is full, it grows first. All elements from the insertion index to the end are shifted one position toward the end to make room. The new element is then copied into the vacated slot. The element count increases by one. Allocation failure terminates the process.
behavior.deletion: An element at a specified index is removed. All elements after the deleted index are shifted one position toward the beginning. The element count decreases by one. The allocated buffer capacity is never reduced. No validation is performed — the index must be within bounds.
behavior.replacement: An element at a specified index is overwritten by copying the new element value into the slot. The element count does not change. The index must be within bounds — no validation is performed.
behavior.clearing: All elements are removed by setting the element count to zero. The allocated buffer is not freed and the capacity is unchanged. After clearing, the array reports zero elements but its capacity remains at whatever it was before.
behavior.element-access: A direct pointer into the buffer at the requested index is returned. The pointer is valid until the next mutation that may reallocate the buffer. The index must be within bounds — no validation is performed.
behavior.size-and-capacity-queries: The current element count and current allocated capacity can each be queried independently. Both return unsigned integer values.
behavior.byte-typed-specialization: A byte-typed specialization of the generic array stores unsigned 8-bit values. It has the same creation, append, insert, delete, clear, and size operations as the generic array, using an element size of one byte. The get operation differs: it returns the byte value directly rather than a pointer into the buffer. There is no capacity query exposed by the byte-typed specialization.

overflow-protection.notable: Integer overflow protection is present for capacity doubling and buffer allocation size, but these limits require storing more elements than any 64-bit system can physically hold (on the order of exabytes). In practice, the process runs out of memory and aborts long before the overflow condition could be reached.
